milestone: Host-aware Bounds, actually enforced -- Scene/Place-hosted content self-heals to full bleed
slice: A direct follow-up to the Hosted-by-Scene z-order fix, triggered by a user screenshot of the real World Builder Inspector showing a Scene-hosted Background Experience's Transform sliders at X:1% Y:29% Width:99% Height:40% -- live, freely-draggable sliders, directly contradicting the Bounds panel's own long-standing copy ('Hosted by Scene -- this Experience fills the whole Scene, read-only'). Root cause: _contentTransformFields() rendered fully editable X/Y/Width/Height sliders for every Experience content section (Text/Image/Graphics) with no check on hostedBy at all -- the Bounds panel's read-only claim was never actually enforced anywhere, letting a Scene- or Place-hosted Experience drift away from full bleed exactly as the screenshot showed, which is the same underlying gap that produced the earlier z-order bug. Fixed by threading exp.hostedBy into _contentTransformFields() at all three call sites (Text/Image/Graphics sections) and gating on it: 'scene'/'place' now render the locked note with zero sliders, and self-heal any already-drifted rect back to full 0/0/1/1 the instant the Inspector opens (no manual slider reset needed) via the same read-time-reconciliation convention this codebase already uses elsewhere (_ensureHolderDefaults, _ordered); 'free' hosting is completely untouched, still fully editable. Verified via a real Playwright reproduction that seeds the user's own exact drifted values, drives the real Workspace UI end to end (Welcome -> open Project -> Scenes strip -> open Scene -> click the Background Experience card) with no manual slider interaction, confirms the stored rect self-heals to full bleed, and runs a real ProjectCompiler.runBuild() confirming the compiled Layer Pack entry now has target:'slide' with a correct full-bleed rect. Full regression across all 17 existing Creator/Builder Playwright scripts, both goldenBuild.js suites, and the Card Platform verify.js all pass unchanged.
